The form *ואדם (wĕʾāḏām)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *אדם (ʾāḏām)* without the definite article. BDB: *אדם (ʾāḏām)* — man, human being; collectively, humankind; as a personal name, Adam. Strong's H120.

In Genesis 2:5, *ʾāḏām* without the article may be functioning as an indefinite common noun: and there was no human. The same word later in the chapter alternates between *hāʾāḏām (the human, with article) and ʾāḏām without article. The word's etymology connects it to *ʾădāmāh (ground/soil)* — human and ground share the same root. The human (ʾāḏām) is the ground-being (ʾădāmāh), and the ground needs the human to work it. Absence of rain and absence of human together explain absence of vegetation — a double dependency embedded in one verse.

אין.explanation:
The particle *אין (ʾayin)* means no, nothing, there is/was no — a negation of existence. BDB: particle of non-existence — there is/was not, no. Strong's H369.

*אין* is one of the most common negative existential particles in Biblical Hebrew. It negates the existence of something: there was no human (*wĕʾāḏām ʾayin*). In Genesis 2:5, it pairs with the preceding *ṭerem* (not yet) to create a comprehensive before-state: rain was not yet, human was not. The same word appears in the famous declaration of God's incomparability: *there is no one like YHWH our God* (Exodus 8:6). *אין* is also used of the void and nothingness from which God creates — *tohu wabohu* (Genesis 1:2) is sometimes read through the lens of *ayin*, the not-yet-existent, though the texts do not explicitly connect them.

לעבד.explanation:
The form *לעבד (laʿăḇōḏ)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + Qal Infinitive Construct of *עבד (ʿāḇaḏ)* — to work, to serve, to till. BDB: to work, to serve, to till — labor on behalf of something or someone. Strong's H5647.

The root *עבד* encompasses both agricultural labor (to work the ground) and serving/worshipping (to serve God). The noun *עבד (ʿeḇeḏ)* means servant or slave. The related noun *עבודה (ʿăḇôḏāh)* means service, worship, or work — the same word used for the priestly service in the tabernacle. In Genesis 2:5 and 15, working the ground (laʿăḇōḏ ʾeṯ hāʾădāmāh) is the first human vocation. This vocation is simultaneously agricultural (tilling) and priestly (serving) — the garden is a sanctuary and the human's work in it is worship.

ואד.explanation:
The noun *אד (ʾêḏ)* means mist, stream, or underground water source. BDB: mist, stream — meaning uncertain; either mist rising from ground or subterranean channel. Strong's H108. The word is rare, appearing only here (Genesis 2:6) and in Job 36:27 (*ʾêḏ*: the water droplets/misty vapor).

Ancient versions translated the word variously: LXX rendered it *pēgē* (spring, source); Vulgate translated it *fons* (spring/fountain). Modern scholarship, influenced by Akkadian cognates (*edu*, a channel or underground watercourse), increasingly translates it as a subterranean stream or artesian spring. The *ʾêḏ* waters the whole face of the ground from below, in contrast with the rain that comes from above (which had not yet fallen, v.5). The pre-rain world had a different irrigation system: bottom-up instead of top-down.

יעלה.explanation:
The form *יעלה (yaʿăleh)* is a Qal Imperfect 3ms of *עלה (ʿālāh)* — to go up, ascend, rise. BDB: to go up, ascend — of persons, things, or vapor rising. Strong's H5927.

The root *עלה (ʿālāh)* is extremely common in the Hebrew Bible — it appears over 890 times. Its basic meaning is upward movement. Here *yaʿăleh* describes the mist rising (*ʿālāh*) from the earth upward, demonstrating that the direction of primordial waters is ascending, from below to above — the reverse of rain. The same root gives the noun *עלה (ʿōlāh)* — burnt offering, the offering that *goes up* to God in smoke. Going up — whether human ascent to God, vapor rising, or sacrifice — is one of the central spatial metaphors of the Hebrew religious imagination.

והשקה.explanation:
The form *והשקה (wĕhišqāh)* is a Hiphil Consecutive Perfect 3ms of *שקה (šāqāh)* — to water, to give drink. The Hiphil is causative: to cause to drink, to irrigate, to water. BDB: Hiphil — to water, to irrigate. Strong's H8248.

The root *שקה* covers both watering plants/animals and providing drink to humans and animals. The same root appears in Genesis 2:10 (the river *lĕhašqôṯ* — to water — the garden) and later in the Joseph story where the cupbearer (*šāqeh*) serves Pharaoh drink (40:1, 21). The priestly vocabulary of serving the sanctuary and the agricultural vocabulary of watering the garden share the same root. The garden is a sanctuary; its irrigation is priestly service.

ויצר.explanation:
The form *ויצר (wayyîṣer)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *יצר (yāṣar)* — to form, to shape, to fashion. BDB: to form, to fashion — as a potter works clay. Strong's H3335.

In the Masoretic text, the word *ויצר (wayyîṣer)* in Genesis 2:7 is written with two *יod* letters (*ייצר*), which the rabbis interpreted as alluding to the double formation: body and soul (the earthly as well as the eternal dimension of the human). The root *יצר (yāṣar)* is the potter's verb — shaping clay on a wheel. In Isaiah 45:9, God is the *yōṣēr* (potter) and humans are the clay. In Jeremiah 18:4, the potter reshapes a marred vessel. The potter imagery frames human createdness as something shaped, held, and potentially reshaped by its Maker.

עפר.explanation:
The noun *עפר (ʿāp̄ār)* means dust, loose dry soil, fine earth — the powdery elementary matter of the ground. BDB: dry earth, dust, loose soil, powder. Strong's H6083.

*עפר* appears at the origin of human life (Genesis 2:7 — dust from the ground) and at its conclusion (Genesis 3:19 — for dust you are and to dust you shall return). The human's substance is *ʿāp̄ār*: dry, loose, elementary, inert without the divine breath. The same word appears in the covenant curse formula (licking the dust, Psalm 72:9) and in the promised expansion of Abraham's descendants (as numerous as the dust of the earth, Genesis 13:16). Dust is simultaneously the most humble and the most numerous substance — the human material is the most basic possible, yet it receives the most intimate divine act: the breath of God.

ויפח.explanation:
The form *ויפח (wayyippāḥ)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *נפח (nāp̄aḥ)* — to blow, to breathe. BDB: to blow, breathe — used of breathing fire into coals, breath into a body. Strong's H5301.

The verb *נפח* describes the physical act of forceful exhalation — blowing. God blows breath into the human's nostrils. This is the most intimate creative act in all of Genesis: God's own breath directly entering the human body through the nostrils. The same root appears in Ezekiel 37:9 when God commands the wind-spirit to breathe (*nĕp̄aḥ*) into the dry bones and bring them to life — the valley of dry bones resurrection scene explicitly echoes Genesis 2:7. Life comes by divine breath.

באפיו.explanation:
The form *באפיו (bĕʾappāw)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* — in, into — + *אף (ʾap̄)* — nose/nostril — in the dual form with a 3ms possessive suffix: into his nostrils. BDB: *אף (ʾap̄)* — nose, nostril (primary); then anger (face reddened with anger). Strong's H639.

The dual form (*ʾappāw*, his two nostrils) reflects the physical anatomy. God breathes into both nostrils simultaneously — an image of complete, direct, anatomically specific divine action. The same word *ʾap̄* means anger in its secondary sense — the nose flares in anger. Both the intimacy of breath and the heat of anger are carried by the same word. The human's nose is where divine life enters and where human emotion is most visibly displayed. The intersection of divine breath and human anger in one body part is not accidental.

נשמת.explanation:
The form *נשמת (nišmaṯ)* is the noun *נשמה (nĕšāmāh)* — breath, the breath within — in construct state: the breath of. BDB: breath — specifically the breath within living beings, the life-breath in the nostrils. Strong's H5397.

*נשמה (nĕšāmāh)* is distinct from *רוח (rûaḥ)* — wind, spirit — that hovered over the waters in Genesis 1:2. *rûaḥ* is the powerful wind-spirit of God moving externally; *nĕšāmāh* is the intimate breath breathed through nostrils into the body. BDB notes that *nĕšāmāh* specifically denotes the breath of life as experienced from within — the living breath of conscious life. In Job 27:3 and 33:4, *nĕšāmāh* is used synonymously with *rûaḥ* in parallel poetry. In Proverbs 20:27, *nĕšamat ʾāḏām* — the breath of a person — is the lamp of YHWH, searching the inner chambers of the being.

חיים.explanation:
The noun *חיים (ḥayyîm)* is the plural of *חי (ḥay)* — alive, living — and means life, lives, living. BDB: life — used as a plural to express the fullness and ongoing vitality of living. Strong's H2416.

*חיים* is consistently used in the plural in Hebrew when referring to life as a vital, ongoing state. *nišmaṯ ḥayyîm (נשמת חיים)* — the breath of life — is the divine gift that makes the human dust-body into a *nep̄eš ḥayyāh* (living soul). The Plural of *ḥayyîm* is always plural in this sense — like water (*mayim*) and heavens (*šāmayim*), it has no singular form when referring to the living state. The *ʿêṣ haḥayyîm (עץ החיים)* — the tree of life (v.9) — also carries this plural, expressing complete, abundant, ongoing life rather than a limited single life-event.

לנפש.explanation:
The form *לנפש (lĕnep̄eš)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* — into/as — + *נפש (nep̄eš)* — soul, being, life. Together: into a [living] soul, as a living soul. BDB: *נפש* — soul, living being, self. Strong's H5315.

In Genesis 2:7, the human *became (wayhî)* a living soul (*lĕnep̄eš ḥayyāh*). The preposition *lĕ* marks the state the human entered: he became unto/as a living soul. This is not the addition of a soul to a body but the transformation of dust-plus-breath into a whole, unified living being. The equivalent phrase *nep̄eš ḥayyāh* (living soul) was already used of animals in 1:20, 24. Here in 2:7 the identical phrase is used for the human — the human belongs to the same category of living-souled being as the animals. The difference is not the soul-category but how the human became one: through personal divine breath.

ויטע.explanation:
The form *ויטע (wayyiṭṭaʿ)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *נטע (nāṭaʿ)* — to plant, to establish. BDB: to plant — of trees, vineyards, and symbolic planting of nations. Strong's H5193.

The root *נטע* is used of deliberate planting — not of seeds germinating spontaneously but of an agent placing plants. YHWH God actively plants the garden *miqqeḏem* (eastward). The same verb is used in Isaiah 51:16 (God plants the heavens) and Jeremiah 18:9 (building and planting nations). In Psalm 44:2, God planted (*nāṭaʿ*) the patriarchs in the land. Planting is a deliberate act of cultivation and presence — God as gardener actively establishes the garden sanctuary before placing the human in it.

גן.explanation:
The noun *גן (gan)* means a garden — specifically an enclosed, cultivated, irrigated space as opposed to open field or wilderness. BDB: garden — an enclosed piece of ground, cultivated. Strong's H1588.

The root behind *גן* is *גנן (gānan)* — to surround, enclose, protect. A garden is fundamentally an enclosed and protected growing space. The Garden of Eden is not the whole landscape but a particular walled or hedged sanctuary within it. The same word appears in the love poetry of Song of Songs (*gannāy*, my garden, 4:16; 5:1; 6:2) where the garden is a symbol of the beloved. The New Testament describes Jesus's tomb as in a garden (*kepōs*, John 19:41) and his resurrection appearance mistaked for the gardener — a deliberate echo of the one who walks in the garden in Genesis 3:8.

בעדן.explanation:
The form *בעדן (bĕʿêḏen)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* — in — + *עדן (ʿêḏen)* — Eden. The place name Eden may derive from *ʿeḏen* — delight, luxury, pleasure — from the root *ʿāḏan*. BDB: Eden — delight; the proper name of the primordial garden. Strong's H5731.

Eden as a place appears in Genesis 2–4, Isaiah 51:3 (YHWH will comfort Zion and make it like Eden), Ezekiel 28:13 (you were in Eden, the garden of God — said to the prince of Tyre), Ezekiel 31:9, 16, 18, and Joel 2:3 (the land before them was like Eden). In all post-Genesis uses, Eden is a memory of original abundance and divine presence — the standard of paradise against which all future restoration or loss is measured. The word's connection to pleasure/delight (*ʿeḏen*) makes it a name that encodes the pre-fall condition of joyful abundance.

מקדם.explanation:
The form *מקדם (miqqeḏem)* is the preposition *מן (min)* + *קדם (qeḏem)* — east, front, ancient times. In spatial usage, *qeḏem* means east (the direction one faces, where the sun rises). BDB: *קדם (qeḏem)* — what is in front, the east, antiquity. Strong's H6924.

East (*qeḏem*) carries sacred orientation in Hebrew cosmology. The tabernacle and temple face east; the high priest enters from the east. Eastward is toward the sunrise, toward the primordial space of divine presence. Planting the garden *miqqeḏem* — in the east — places the divine sanctuary at the cosmic sunrise-point. After the fall, the human is driven east of Eden (3:24), away from the divine presence. Cain wanders east of Eden (4:16). East is simultaneously the direction of the sacred origin and the direction of exile.

וישם.gloss: and he put, and he placed
וישם.explanation:
The form *וישם (wayyāśem)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *שים (śîm)* or *שום (śûm)* — to put, to set, to place. BDB: to put, to set, to place, to appoint. Strong's H7760.

The root *שים/שום* is one of the most common verbs of placement and appointment in the Hebrew Bible. Here God places (*wayyāśem*) the human in the garden he has just planted. The sequence — God plants the garden, then places the human — is deliberate: the garden is prepared first, then the gardener is installed. The same verb is used for placing Joseph over Egypt (Genesis 41:41), placing Israel as a name-bearer (Isaiah 49:3), and placing law before Israel (Exodus 21:1). Placement is always purposeful and defining.

יצר.explanation:
The form *יצר (yāṣār)* is a Qal Perfect 3ms of *יצר (yāṣar)* — to form, to shape. This is the simple Perfect, not the Consecutive Imperfect, indicating a completed action in the past: he had formed. BDB: Qal perfect of *יצר* — to form, to fashion, to shape. Strong's H3335.

In Genesis 2:8, *ʾăšer yāṣār* — whom he had formed — refers back to the forming act of verse 7. The relative clause connects the garden placement (v.8) to the prior formation (v.7): God placed in the garden the human whom he had already formed. The perfect tense in the relative clause signals this temporal sequence: formation preceded placement. The potter-God forms the human, then places the formed human in the sanctuary-garden — the sequence of creation followed by vocation-assignment.

ויצמח.gloss: and he made to spring up
ויצמח.explanation:
The form *ויצמח (wayyaṣmaḥ)* is a Hiphil Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *צמח (ṣāmaḥ)* — the Hiphil causative: and he caused to sprout/spring up. BDB: Hiphil of *צמח* — to cause to grow, to make sprout. Strong's H6779.

In Genesis 2:9, YHWH God causes (*wayyaṣmaḥ*) every tree to spring up from the ground. This is different from verse 5 (no plant had yet sprung up, *yiṣmāḥ*, Qal intransitive). Now God actively causes growth — the Hiphil marks YHWH God's agency in producing the garden's trees. The same Hiphil appears in Isaiah 61:11 (as the earth makes plants to sprout, YHWH will cause righteousness to sprout before all nations) — the vegetation-sprouting imagery becomes a metaphor for divine salvation-action.

נחמד.explanation:
The form *נחמד (neḥmāḏ)* is a Niphal Participle masculine singular of *חמד (ḥāmaḏ)* — to desire, covet, delight in. As a Niphal passive participle: being desired, desirable, pleasant. BDB: Niphal participle of *חמד* — desirable, pleasant, attractive. Strong's H2530.

The root *חמד* covers both legitimate delight (the desirable are beautiful and pleasing) and forbidden covetousness. The tenth commandment uses the Qal of *חמד*: do not *covet* your neighbor's house, wife, etc. (Exodus 20:17). In Genesis 2:9, the trees are *neḥmāḏ lĕmarʾeh* — desirable to the sight — using the exact same root that will appear in 3:6 when the woman sees the tree of knowledge as *neḥmāḏ lĕhaśkîl* — desirable to make one wise. The desire planted in the garden as God's gift becomes the desire weaponized by the serpent. *ḥāmaḏ* is morally neutral — the goodness or evil of desire depends on its object.

למראה.explanation:
The form *למראה (lĕmarʾeh)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + *מראה (marʾeh)* — sight, appearance, vision. BDB: *מראה (marʾeh)* — sight, appearance, what is seen; from the root *ראה (rāʾāh)* — to see. Strong's H4758.

The phrase *neḥmāḏ lĕmarʾeh (נחמד למראה)* — desirable to the sight/appearance — describes the aesthetic dimension of the trees. Creation in Genesis 1 was declared good (functional); now in Genesis 2:9 the trees are declared desirable to see (beautiful). Beauty as a category appears in the garden narrative. The same word *marʾeh* is used of Joseph's appearance (very handsome, *ṭôḇ marʾeh*, Genesis 39:6), of the beautiful daughters of men (Genesis 6:2), and of prophetic visions (*marʾeh*). Sight and vision are the organs of encounter with beauty and revelation.

למאכל.explanation:
The form *למאכל (lĕmaʾăḵāl)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + *מאכל (maʾăḵāl)* — food, edible things. BDB: *מאכל* — food. From the root *אכל (ʾāḵal)* — to eat. Strong's H3978.

The trees are doubly qualified: *neḥmāḏ lĕmarʾeh wĕṭôḇ lĕmaʾăḵāl* — desirable to the sight and good for food. Beauty and utility together describe the ideal. Genesis 1:29–30 had already established the food-granting at the end of the sixth day. Now Genesis 2:9 specifies that the garden trees are both aesthetically pleasing and edible. In 3:6, the woman re-evaluates the forbidden tree using the same vocabulary: *ṭôḇ...lĕmaʾăḵāl* (good for food) — the identical phrase from 2:9. The temptation narrative explicitly echoes the garden's original vocabulary of beauty and food.

הגן.explanation:
The form *הגן (haggān)* is the definite article *ה (ha)* + *גן (gan)* — the garden. BDB: *גן* — garden, enclosed cultivated space. Strong's H1588.

With the definite article, *הגן* — the garden — has become a known, specific entity: the Garden of Eden. The article marks the garden as previously identified and uniquely referential. After its introduction in 2:8 (*gan bĕʿêḏen*), subsequent references simply say *hag·gān* — the garden — treating it as established shared knowledge between narrator and reader. The same word without the article (*gan ʿêḏen*) and the specific form *gannô* (his garden) appear in later contexts, but *haggān* (the garden) is the primary reference for the Eden sanctuary throughout chapters 2–3.

הדעת.explanation:
The form *הדעת (hadda·ʿaṯ)* is the definite article *ה (ha)* + *דעת (daʿaṯ)* — knowledge, knowing, intimate experience. BDB: *דעת (daʿaṯ)* — knowledge, wisdom, discernment — from the root *ידע (yāḏaʿ)* — to know. Strong's H1847.

The root *ידע (yāḏaʿ)* covers intellectual knowledge, experiential knowledge, and intimate relational knowing. In Genesis 4:1, Adam *knew (yāḏaʿ)* his wife — sexual intimacy. In Exodus 6:3, God says the patriarchs did not *know (yāḏaʿ)* him by his name YHWH — covenantal intimate knowledge. *daʿaṯ ṭôḇ wārāʿ* — knowledge of good and evil — is comprehensive moral experience: knowing the full range of good and evil from personal experiential participation. The tree offers experiential initiation into the complete moral spectrum.

ורע.explanation:
The form *ורע (wārāʿ)* is *ו (wĕ)* + *רע (rāʿ)* — evil, bad, wicked. BDB: *רע (rāʿ)* — evil, bad, wicked — both moral evil and physical harm/misfortune. Strong's H7451.

The pairing *ṭôḇ wārāʿ (טוב ורע)* — good and evil — is a merism: the complete moral range from one extreme to the other, encompassing everything between them. This merism captures the total moral knowledge the tree represents: not just knowing some good and some evil, but the comprehensive moral reality of existence. The same pairing appears in 2 Samuel 14:17 (the king is like an angel of God, knowing good and evil) and in Deuteronomy 1:39 (children who do not yet know good or evil — used of moral innocence). The knowledge of the full moral range was reserved for God alone; the tree offered to share it.

ונהר.explanation:
The noun *נהר (nāhār)* means river — a flowing body of water. BDB: river, stream. Strong's H5104.

In Genesis 2:10, the river originates inside Eden and flows outward to water the garden, then divides into four rivers. This is the reverse of the normal geographic pattern where rivers have their sources outside a region and flow into it. The garden-river flows from the inside out — the divine sanctuary is the source of the world's water. Isaiah 33:21 (YHWH will be a place of rivers for us) and Ezekiel 47:1–12 (the river flowing from the temple) develop this image: the *nāhār* of Eden becomes the eschatological river of healing water flowing from the restored divine sanctuary. Revelation 22:1–2 completes the arc with the river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and the Lamb.

יצא.explanation:
The form *יצא (yōṣêʾ)* here is a Qal Participle masculine singular of *יצא (yāṣāʾ)* — to go out, to come out, to flow out. As a participle, it describes ongoing or habitual action: a river going out / flowing out. BDB: to go out, come out, come forth. Strong's H3318.

The root *יצא* is one of the most frequent Hebrew verbs of motion — appearing over 1000 times. The Exodus itself is called *yĕṣîʾāṯ miṣrayim* (the going out from Egypt), using the same root. The river *yōṣêʾ* from Eden — going out from the divine sanctuary — anticipates a theological pattern: what comes out from the divine presence waters and sustains the world outside. The Exodus uses the same verb: Israel going out from Egypt parallels the river going out from Eden — release from bondage to sustained life in freedom.

מעדן.explanation:
The form *מעדן (mêʿêḏen)* is the preposition *מן (min)* — from — + *עדן (ʿêḏen)* — Eden. BDB: *עדן* — Eden, delight. Strong's H5731.

The river flows *mêʿêḏen* — from Eden — outward into the world. Eden as the divine-garden sanctuary is the geographic fountainhead of all the world's primary rivers in this cosmology. The source of life-giving water is the divine presence, not some neutral natural feature. This motif is developed in Ezekiel 47 (water flowing from the temple threshold eastward) and Zechariah 14:8 (living waters flowing from Jerusalem) and Revelation 22:1 (the river of the water of life... flowing from the throne of God). The theological logic is consistent: wherever God dwells, life flows outward from that presence.

ומשם.explanation:
The form *ומשם (ūmiššām)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *מ (min)* — from — + *שם (šām)* — there. Combined: and from there. BDB: *שם* — there, in that place. Strong's H8033.

*ומשם* marks the transition from single river (flowing from Eden into the garden) to the four-river division: from there [the garden] it divided into four heads. The garden is both the destination of the Eden-river (it waters the garden) and the source-point of the four world-rivers (from there they divide). The garden is thus the world's geographic center — the point from which all rivers of the habitable world radiate. This is the cosmological equivalent of Jerusalem as the center of the earth (Ezekiel 38:12: *ṭabbûr hāʾāreṣ*, navel of the earth).

יפרד.explanation:
The form *יפרד (yippārêḏ)* is a Niphal Imperfect 3ms of *פרד (pāraḏ)* — to divide, separate, part. The Niphal is passive/reflexive: it divided itself, it became divided. BDB: Niphal — to be divided, separated, dispersed. Strong's H6504.

The root *פרד* describes not a forced external separation but a natural self-dividing. The river divides into four streams from the single source. The same root appears in Genesis 13:9 (let us separate from each other — Abraham and Lot), Genesis 13:11 (they separated), and Ruth 1:17 (only death will separate us — using the Hiphil). *פרד* is theologically loaded: separation from the divine center (Eden) is always a theme in Genesis. The river's division from Eden foreshadows the human's exile from Eden — both involve the spreading out from the divine center into the wider, more fragmented world.

לארבעה.explanation:
The form *לארבעה (lĕʾarḇāʿāh)* is the preposition *לְ (lĕ)* — into/for — + *ארבעה (ʾarḇāʿāh)* — four (masculine form before masculine noun). BDB: *ארבע/ארבעה (ʾarbaʿ/ʾarḇāʿāh)* — four. Strong's H702.

Four (ʾarbaʿ) is the number of the world's directions — north, south, east, west — and therefore symbolically represents completeness in geographic scope. Four rivers flowing from Eden to the four corners of the world means Eden is the source of life for all of creation in all directions. The same symbolism appears in Ezekiel 1 (four living creatures, four faces, four wings — celestial creatures representing all living kinds in all directions) and Revelation 7:1 (four angels at the four corners of the earth). *Four* is the number of cosmic comprehensiveness.

ראשים.explanation:
The form *ראשים (rāšîm)* is the plural of *ראש (rōʾš)* — head, top, chief, beginning. BDB: heads — of rivers, meaning their main branches or tributaries. Strong's H7218.

The plural *rāšîm* (heads) is used for river branches — the main streams into which the single Eden-river divides. This follows the ancient geographic vocabulary where the sources or main branches of a river are called its heads. The same word is used for chiefs, headmen, heads of lists, and calendar beginnings (*rōʾš haššānāh* — the head of the year, New Year). The use of *rāšîm* for the four river branches creates an echo with the chapter's central figure: the human is made from the head/top of creation, placed at the center of the garden, and named by his *ראש (rōʾš)* — head — the beginning/most important part. Humanity is the ʾādām at the rōʾš of everything.

ויקח.explanation:
The form *ויקח (wayyiqqaḥ)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *לקח (lāqaḥ)* — to take, to receive. BDB: to take, to grasp, to receive, to collect. Strong's H3947.

In Genesis 2:15, God takes (*wayyiqqaḥ*) the human and places him in the garden. In 2:21, God takes (*wayyiqqaḥ*) one of the human's ribs. In 2:22, God takes from the human (*lāqaḥ*). The verb *lāqaḥ* appears three times in Chapter 2 with God as subject — three acts of divine taking that shape the garden narrative. Taking in the Hebrew Bible carries no implication of force; it is simply the act of seizing or receiving. The same root provides the word *miqdāš* in Exodus 25:8 — take for me an offering (*wĕqāḥū lî tĕrûmāh*) — Israel's material for the sanctuary, to be taken and offered to God.

וינחהו.gloss: and he put him, and he settled him
וינחהו.explanation:
The form *וינחהו (wayyannîḥêhū)* is a Hiphil Consecutive Imperfect 3ms + 3ms suffix of *נוח (nûaḥ)* — to rest, to settle, to cause to rest. The Hiphil causative: and he caused him to rest/settled him. BDB: Hiphil of *נוח* — to put, to set, to let rest, to deposit. Strong's H3240.

The root *נוח (nûaḥ)* gives us *nûaḥ* (Noah) — whose name means rest or comfort in a punned etymology (Genesis 5:29). God's act of settling the human in the garden uses the same root as the Sabbath rest (šabbat, the cessation) and Noah's name. Resting and settling are connected: God places the human in the garden-sanctuary as a settled dweller, not a transient visitor. The Hiphil causative makes God the agent of the settling — God installs the human in the garden as the temple-king installs priests in the sanctuary.

בגן.explanation:
The form *בגן (ḇĕḡan)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* — in — + *גן (gan)* — garden — in construct state: in the garden of. BDB: *גן* — garden. Strong's H1588.

The construct form *ḇĕḡan-ʿêḏen* — in the garden of Eden — appears in Genesis 2:15. The preposition *ב (bĕ)* specifies location; the construct chain *gan-ʿêḏen* names the specific garden (of Eden). The same form appears in 3:1, 8, 10, 23, 24. The garden with its divine presence is the spatial center of the narrative. After the expulsion (3:23–24), the human is outside *gan-ʿêḏen* — the loss of location is the loss of the divine presence-sanctuary. Geography and theology coincide: to be in the garden is to be with God; to be expelled from it is to be separated.

עדן.explanation:
The proper noun *עדן (ʿêḏen)* — Eden — appears here without a preposition in construct with *גן*: garden of Eden. BDB: *עדן (ʿêḏen)* — delight, luxury; the proper name of the primordial garden. Strong's H5731.

Without the preposition (unlike *בעדן* in v.8), Eden here functions as the pure genitive in the construct chain: the garden-of-Eden. The word *ʿêḏen* may derive from a root meaning delight, luxury, or pleasure — resonant with the garden's abundance. Later, *ʿeḏānîm* (luxuries, delights) appears as a cognate. The Septuagint consistently translates Eden as *paradeisos* (paradise, from Old Iranian *pairidaeza*, an enclosed royal garden) — giving the Western world the word paradise for the garden of God. Every subsequent use of the idea of paradise in Jewish, Christian, and Islamic traditions ultimately derives from this Hebrew word.

לעבדה.explanation:
The form *לעבדה (lĕʿāḇĕḏāh)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + Qal Infinitive Construct of *עבד (ʿāḇaḏ)* + 3fs suffix (referring to the garden): to work it, to serve it. BDB: Qal infinitive of *עבד* — to work, to till, to serve. Strong's H5647.

The agricultural meaning (to till the garden) and the priestly meaning (to serve the sanctuary) are both present in *lĕʿāḇĕḏāh*. Commentators such as Gordon Wenham and John Walton have extensively documented that Numbers 3:7–8 and 18:7 use *ʿāḇaḏ (to serve)* and *šāmar (to keep/guard)* together precisely to describe the Levitical duties in the tabernacle. The human in the garden is a proto-Levite: his vocation of working and keeping the garden-sanctuary is the prototype for all priestly service. The garden is the first temple; the human is the first priest.

ולשמרה.gloss: and to keep it, to guard it
ולשמרה.explanation:
The form *ולשמרה (ûlĕšāmĕrāh)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *ל (lĕ)* — to — + Qal Infinitive Construct of *שמר (šāmar)* + 3fs suffix: and to keep/guard it. BDB: Qal infinitive of *שמר* — to keep, to guard, to observe, to take care of. Strong's H8104.

The root *שמר* covers guarding (military), observing (ritual), and keeping (relational). All three senses are active in the garden vocation: guard the sanctuary, observe its sacred regulations, and maintain its relational integrity. The same root underlies *šemîrāh* (watch, guard) and the Shema (*šĕmaʿ*, hear and observe). In Numbers 6:24, the Aaronic blessing: *YHWH bless you and keep you (yišmĕreḵā)*. The human's vocation of keeping the garden is the prototype of the priestly blessing — to be kept and to keep are the same root. Humanity as keeper of the garden later becomes humanity kept by God's priestly blessing.

ויצו.explanation:
The form *ויצו (wayyĕṣaw)* is a Piel Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *צוה (ṣāwāh)* — to command, to charge, to commission. BDB: Piel of *צוה* — to command, to charge, to appoint, to give instruction. Strong's H6680.

Genesis 2:16 marks the first time God issues a command to a human being. The root *צוה* and its derived noun *miṣwāh (commandment)* become the defining vocabulary of the Torah: the 613 commandments (*miṣwôṯ*). This first command comes in the garden and establishes the framework of commanded freedom: God commands the full scope of permission (every tree) and the single restriction (one tree). The garden command is the prototype of all Torah-commands: comprehensive generosity with defined limits. The verb appears over 490 times in the Hebrew Bible — the garden is where commanding begins.

לאמר.explanation:
The form *לאמר (lêmōr)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + Qal Infinitive Construct of *אמר (ʾāmar)* — to say. As a formulaic phrase: saying, as follows. BDB: *לאמר* — saying — a standard formula introducing direct speech after verbs of speaking or commanding. Strong's H559.

*לאמר (lêmōr)* appears hundreds of times in the Hebrew Bible as a quotation marker: God said [to them] *lêmōr*, saying, and then the quoted speech follows. It appears after *wayĕṣaw (commanded)* in 2:16, introducing the garden command as direct divine speech. The formula emphasizes the direct, oral quality of divine communication: God does not issues abstract decrees but speaks words — *lêmōr* — that can be quoted back. Moses uses the same formula throughout Deuteronomy when recounting God's words to Israel.

תאכל.gloss: you may eat, you shall eat
תאכל.explanation:
The form *תאכל (tōḵêl)* is a Qal Imperfect 2ms of *אכל (ʾāḵal)* — to eat — you shall eat, you may eat. Combined with the preceding *ʾāḵōl* (Infinitive Absolute), it creates the emphatic *ʾāḵōl tōḵêl* — eating you shall eat — certain permission. BDB: Qal imperfect of *אכל* — to eat, to consume. Strong's H398.

The pairing *ʾāḵōl tōḵêl (אכל תאכל)* is the infinitive absolute + finite verb construction that emphasizes certainty and completeness. You shall certainly eat / you may freely eat. The same construction appears in 2:17 as the death warning (*môṯ tāmûṯ* — you shall surely die), making permission and prohibition structurally parallel: the same grammatical emphasis marks both the full permission and the absolute prohibition. Language itself is being used to create equal weight on the two sides of the garden command.

ומעץ.explanation:
The form *ומעץ (ûmêʿêṣ)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and/but — + *מן (min)* — from — + *עץ (ʿêṣ)* — tree: and from the tree of. BDB: *עץ* — tree, wood, timber. Strong's H6086.

The connective *ו (wĕ)* here has a contrastive force — but. After the broad permission of 2:16 (from all the trees of the garden you may eat), the contrast is introduced: but from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil... The contrast structure (all / but one) defines the shape of human freedom: nearly unlimited range, with one defined exception. This structure reappears throughout the Torah: Israel may do all things, but not these (the commandment list). The garden command is the prototype of Torah-logic.

ממנו.explanation:
The form *ממנו (mimmenū)* is the preposition *מן (min)* — from — + 3ms suffix: from it, of it. BDB: *מן* with 3ms suffix — from it. Strong's H4480.

In Genesis 2:17, *lōʾ tōḵal mimmenū* — you shall not eat of it — and then *bĕyôm ʾăḵālĕḵā mimmenū* — in the day you eat of it. The preposition appears twice with *it* referring to the tree of knowledge. The repetition of the same construction (eat of it / when you eat of it) creates a tight grammatical echo between prohibition and consequence: the very act prohibited (*eat of it*) is the condition that triggers the consequence (*die*). Syntax mirrors theology: the prohibited act and its consequence are grammatically bound together.

מות.explanation:
The form *מות (môṯ)* is a Qal Infinitive Absolute of *מות (mûṯ)* — to die. Used with the finite verb *tāmûṯ* (you shall die) in the emphatic construction *môṯ tāmûṯ*: dying you shall die / you shall surely die. BDB: *מות (môṯ)* — to die; infinitive absolute used to intensify the following verb. Strong's H4191.

The emphatic *môṯ tāmûṯ* matches the emphatic *ʾāḵōl tōḵêl* of verse 16 exactly in grammatical structure: the same infinitive absolute + finite verb construction. The certainty of freedom (you shall freely eat) and the certainty of consequence (you shall surely die) are grammatically mirror-images. The serpent in 3:4 will specifically deny this: *môṯ lōʾ tāmûṯûn* — you shall certainly not die — using the same construction in negation. The serpent's denial echoes the grammar of God's warning, making its denial even more audacious.

תמות.explanation:
The form *תמות (tāmûṯ)* is a Qal Imperfect 2ms of *מות (mûṯ)* — to die: you shall die, you will die. BDB: Qal imperfect of *מות* — to die. Strong's H4191.

Paired with the Infinitive Absolute *môṯ* before it, the finite verb *tāmûṯ* creates the certain death warning: dying-dying you/you shall. The root *מות* appears throughout Genesis as the ultimate consequence of sin and violence. After the fall, God announces mortality will be the human condition (3:19). After Cain's murder, blood cries from the ground. Before the flood, violence fills the earth as death multiplies. The first death-warning in the Bible — *môṯ tāmûṯ* — initiates a trajectory of mortality that runs through Jewish scripture and is theologically engaged by Paul in Romans 5 and 1 Corinthians 15: where death came through one act of disobedience, life comes through one act of obedience.

ביום.gloss: in the day, on the day
ביום.explanation:
The form *ביום (bĕyôm)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* — in, on — + *יום (yôm)* — day. Together: on the day, in the day, when. BDB: *יום (yôm)* — day, period of time, moment. Strong's H3117.

*bĕyôm* in 2:17 introduces the conditional time clause: *bĕyôm ʾăḵālĕḵā mimmenū* — on the day that you eat of it [you shall die]. The *bĕyôm* construction is often used as a temporal conjunction — when/at the time that. This is important for the interpretation of the death warning: the human does not die on the literal calendar day of eating (Genesis 3 narrates ongoing life after the fall). Ancient and modern interpreters have read *bĕyôm* as: (1) immediately that day (literal), requiring either a meaning of immediate spiritual death or an extended calendar; (2) at the moment when — i.e., mortality becomes your condition from that point; or (3) as a beginning of the death process.

אכלך.explanation:
The form *אכלך (ʾăḵālĕḵā)* is the Qal Infinitive Construct of *אכל (ʾāḵal)* — to eat — with a 2ms suffix: your eating, when you eat. In context: in the day of your eating from it. BDB: Qal infinitive construct of *אכל* — to eat. Strong's H398.

The Infinitive Construct + subject suffix (*ʾăḵālĕḵā mimmenū*) creates a subordinate temporal clause: on the day of-your-eating from it. This construction is common in Hebrew for expressing conditional time: when you eat. The suffix *-ḵā* (you, 2ms) embedded directly in the infinitive makes the action personally specific: not eating in general but *your* eating — this particular individual human at this particular tree. The command is personal, addressed directly to a specific person, not a general principle.

היות.explanation:
The form *היות (hĕyôṯ)* is a Qal Infinitive Construct of *היה (hāyāh)* — to be, to exist. Used with *lōʾ ṭôḇ*: it is not good for [the man] to be alone. BDB: Qal infinitive construct of *היה* — to be, to become, to exist. Strong's H1961.

The phrase *lōʾ ṭôḇ hĕyôṯ hāʾāḏām lĕḇaddô* — it is not good for the man to be alone — uses the infinitive *hĕyôṯ* in a subject clause: the-being of the man alone is not good. The same root *היה (hāyāh)* underlies the divine name *YHWH* (he who is / he who causes to be). The first not-good in Genesis describes the relational aloneness of the being (*hĕyôṯ* — existence, being) who bears the image of the God who IS (*YHWH*). Aloneness is not the condition of the God who is inherently relational; therefore aloneness is not good for the image-bearer either.

לבדו.explanation:
The form *לבדו (lĕḇaddô)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + *בד (baḏ)* — separation, isolation — + 3ms suffix: in his isolation, alone. BDB: *לבד (lĕḇaḏ)* — alone, by oneself, apart. Strong's H905.

*לבד* describes separation and aloneness. It appears in contexts of solitary existence: Elijah believing he is the only prophet left, alone (*lĕḇaddî*, 1 Kings 19:10, 14). The phrase *lĕḇaddô* (alone, by himself) in 2:18 is the first divine diagnosis of a deficiency in the created order. The creation of Genesis 1 was progressively good; now something in the relational structure of human existence is not-good. The correction — making a helper corresponding to him — reestablishes the relational completeness that aloneness had broken. The diagnosis of human aloneness as not-good makes human sociality — the capacity for genuine mutual relation — a theological necessity.

אעשה.explanation:
The form *אעשה (ʾeʿĕśeh)* is a Qal Imperfect 1cs of *עשה (ʿāśāh)* — I will make, I will do. BDB: Qal imperfect of *עשה* — to make, to do. Strong's H6213.

In Genesis 2:18, *ʾeʿĕśeh-lô ʿêzer kĕneḡdô* — I will make for him a helper corresponding to him — is God's first-person resolution to the not-good diagnosis. God commits to action. The same verb *ʿāśāh* was used throughout Genesis 1 for God's creating work. Now God will make (*ʾeʿĕśeh*) something to address the relational gap. The choice of *ʿāśāh* (make, craft) rather than *bārāʾ* (create, bring into being uniquely) marks the woman's creation as a making — a crafting from existing material (the rib) — consistent with the distributional pattern of Genesis 1.

לו.explanation:
The form *לו (lōw)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* — for, to — + 3ms suffix: for him, to him. BDB: *ל (lĕ)* — preposition indicating direction, possession, reference. Strong's (preposition, no individual number).

In Genesis 2:18, *ʾeʿĕśeh-lô ʿêzer* — I will make for him a helper — the *lô* (for him) specifies the beneficiary of God's making. The helper is not made independently but *for him* — relationally oriented toward the human from the start. The same *lô* appears in verse 20 (*lōʾ māṣāʾ ʿêzer kĕneḡdô* — there was not found a helper corresponding to him), creating a bracket: God intends to make a helper *for him* (v.18); the animal parade confirms no sufficient helper existed *for him* (v.20); God then makes the woman.

עזר.explanation:
The noun *עזר (ʿêzer)* means helper, aid, one who gives assistance. BDB: help, assistance; one who helps. Strong's H5828.

In the Psalms, *ʿêzer* is predominantly used for God as Israel's helper: *my help (ʿezrî) comes from YHWH, maker of heaven and earth* (Psalm 121:2); *our help (ʿezrênû) is in the name of YHWH, maker of heaven and earth* (Psalm 124:8). God is the paradigmatic *ʿêzer*. When God declares he will make a helper (*ʿêzer*) for the human, he is using the vocabulary of his own most characteristic role. The woman is named with God's own title. This decisively argues against interpreting *ʿêzer kĕneḡdô* as a subordinate role — the Helper in the Psalms is always the stronger party rescuing the weaker.

כנגדו.gloss: fit for him, corresponding to him
כנגדו.explanation:
The form *כנגדו (kĕneḡdô)* is the preposition *כ (kĕ)* — like, as — + *נגד (neḡeḏ)* — opposite, front, corresponding — + 3ms suffix: like-facing-him, corresponding to him, a counterpart who faces him. BDB: *נגד (neḡeḏ)* — in front of, corresponding to. Strong's H5048.

*כנגדו* is a prepositional phrase meaning the one who faces/corresponds to/mirrors him. The preposition *כ (kĕ)* indicates similarity — like one facing him — while *neḡeḏ* places the correspondence in spatial terms: the one who stands opposite him, facing him. This is not an identical copy or a subordinate appendage but a corresponding counterpart — the mirror image of the human, who stands facing him with equivalent status. Rabbinic commentary consistently emphasizes the mutuality of *kĕneḡdô*: corresponding means equal in status and worth, though different in role and form.

ויבא.explanation:
The form *ויבא (wayyāḇêʾ)* is a Hiphil Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *בוא (bûʾ)* — to come, to bring (Hiphil causative: to cause to come/bring). BDB: Hiphil of *בוא* — to bring, to bring in, to lead. Strong's H935.

God brings (*wayyāḇêʾ*) the animals to the human in verse 19, and then brings (*wayyĕḇiʾehā*) the woman to the man in verse 22. Both the animal-naming parade and the woman-presentation use the same Hiphil of *bûʾ* — God actively brings both. The symmetry is deliberate: after the animals are paraded and found insufficient (no helper *kĕneḡdô* was found), God brings the woman — and the man's joyful exclamation (at last, bone of my bones!) signals that the search is over. The same God who brought the animals now brings the sufficient counterpart.

לראות.explanation:
The form *לראות (lirʾôṯ)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + Qal Infinitive Construct of *ראה (rāʾāh)* — to see: in order to see, to see. BDB: Qal infinitive of *ראה* — to see, to look, to observe. Strong's H7200.

God brings the animals *lirʾôṯ* — to see — what the man would call them. God's seeing here is not omniscient observation but relational attention — God watches to see what the human will do. This is the first scene in Genesis where God watches a human's action before it happens, allowing the human's choice to unfold. It anticipates the dynamic of Genesis 22 (*now I know that you fear God*) — God creates space for human agency and watches to see what it produces. The human's naming is an exercise of delegated authority that God respectfully observes.

מה.explanation:
The interrogative pronoun *מה (mah)* means what — used in questions and indirect questions. BDB: what? how? why? — the standard interrogative pronoun. Strong's H4100.

*mah* in 2:19 introduces an indirect question: to see *what* he would call them. It is parallel to the direct question form used in naming scenes elsewhere in the Bible. The indirect *mah* here keeps God's perspective external: God does not pre-determine the name; God watches to see *what the human will choose to call*. Human naming is genuinely open — the human's choices are real and not pre-scripted. This is the first scene of genuine human agency and creativity in the narrative.

יקרא.explanation:
The form *יקרא (yiqrāʾ)* is a Qal Imperfect 3ms of *קרא (qārāʾ)* — to call, to name, to summon. In the indirect speech of 2:19 it is a *jussive with indirect speech* or simply the imperfect expressing an open future from the past: whatever he calls / would call. BDB: Qal imperfect of *קרא* — to call, to summon, to proclaim, to name. Strong's H7121.

Naming (*qārāʾ šêm*) is a powerful act in the Hebrew Bible: naming establishes the identity and nature of the named, and the namer exercises authority over the named. God names the fundamental categories in Genesis 1 (light, darkness, sky, earth, sea). Now the human names the animals — delegated naming authority. In 2:23, the human also names the woman (*ʾiššāh*). The sequence: God names, humans name — establishes the pattern of delegated creative authority.

שמו.explanation:
The form *שמו (šĕmô)* is the noun *שם (šêm)* — name — with a 3ms possessive suffix: his name, its name. BDB: *שם (šêm)* — name, reputation, memorial. Strong's H8034.

*šêm (שם)* — name — is one of the most theologically consequential words in the Hebrew Bible. A being's name is its identity, character, and reputation. To declare someone's name is to declare their nature. God's revealed names (YHWH, ʾĕlōhîm, ʾĒl Šadday) are acts of divine self-disclosure. Human naming (Adam names Eve, Abraham is renamed, Jacob becomes Israel, Joshua is renamed from Hoshea) marks identity-defining moments. In 2:19, *hû šĕmô* — that is its name — confirms the authority of the human's naming: whatever the human declares the name to be, it *is* the name. Human creative speech is granted real, lasting effect.

ויקרא.explanation:
The form *ויקרא (wayyiqrāʾ)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *קרא (qārāʾ)* — and he called/named. This is the same form used in Genesis 1:5 (God called the light Day) and 1:8 (God called the expanse Heaven). BDB: Qal consecutive imperfect — and he called. Strong's H7121.

In 2:20, *wayyiqrāʾ hāʾāḏām šêmôṯ* — and the man gave names — the human exercises the same naming authority that was God's in Genesis 1. The consecutive imperfect form (wayyiqtol) marks this as narrative action: a completed naming exercise. The use of the identical form for both God's naming (Genesis 1) and the human's naming (Genesis 2) is arguably deliberate: the human images the divine by doing what God does — naming and ordering creation.

שמות.explanation:
The form *שמות (šêmôṯ)* is the plural of *שם (šêm)* — names. BDB: *שמות (šêmôṯ)* — names. Strong's H8034.

The plural *šêmôṯ* — names — marks the scope of the naming exercise: the human gave names (plural) to all livestock, all birds, and all field animals. Each creature received a specific individual name. The plurality of names reflects the plurality and diversity of the created animal world: the same creative God who made one human made many distinct animal kinds, each requiring its own unique name. The naming produces linguistic diversity corresponding to biological diversity. The same word *šêmôṯ* is the title of the second book of the Torah — Shemot (Exodus/Names) — because it begins with a list of the names of the sons of Israel.

לכל.explanation:
The form *לכל (lĕḵāl)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* — to, for — + *כל (kol)* — all, every. Together: to/for all. BDB: *כל (kol)* — all, every, the whole. Strong's H3605.

In Genesis 2:20, *wayyiqrāʾ hāʾāḏām šêmôṯ lĕḵāl habbĕhêmāh* — and the man gave names to all the livestock — *lĕḵāl habbĕhêmāh* specifies the comprehensive scope: not some livestock but all livestock. The preposition *ל* with *kol* (all) + definite noun signals universal naming: every member of the category received a name. The universality of the naming echoes God's comprehensive creation: God made all kinds; the human named all kinds.

ולעוף.explanation:
The form *ולעוף (ûlĕʾôp̄)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *ל (lĕ)* — to — + *עוף (ʿôp̄)* — birds, flying creatures: and to the birds of. BDB: *עוף (ʿôp̄)* — birds, flying creatures. Strong's H5775.

The form connects to the root *עוף (ʿûp̄)* — to fly. Birds are defined by their flying. In Genesis 1:20–21, birds were created on day 5. In 2:20, the naming exercise includes birds (*ûlĕʾôp̄ haššāmāyim*) — the birds of the heavens. This matches the animals God formed from the ground in verse 19: ground-formed animals (field beasts, but also in this list the birds) all receive names. The naming of creatures from the ground (ʾădāmāh) by the human who came from the ground (ʾāḏām from ʾădāmāh) creates a ground-solidarity: same origin, different creature-types, all named.

ולכל.gloss: and to all, and to every
ולכל.explanation:
The form *ולכל (ûlĕḵōl)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *ל (lĕ)* — to — + *כל (kol)* — all, every. Together: and to all, and to every. BDB: *כל (kol)* — all, every. Strong's H3605.

Appearing twice in verse 20, *ûlĕḵōl* extends the naming scope to cover all categories: and to all livestock (lĕḵāl), and to the birds (ûlĕʾôp̄ haššāmāyim), and to all field beasts (*ûlĕḵōl ḥayyat haśśāḏeh*). The repeated *ûlĕḵōl* with different noun categories creates a comprehensive list construction: all + category-1, all + category-2, all + category-3. This is the Hebrew equivalent of 'all animals everywhere.' The naming covers the complete animal world of Genesis 1:24–25 (land animals: livestock, ground creepers, wild beasts) plus the birds of 1:20–21.

ולאדם.explanation:
The form *ולאדם (ûlĕʾāḏām)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and/but — + *ל (lĕ)* — for — + *ʾāḏām (אדם)* without the definite article. BDB: *אדם (ʾāḏām)* — man, human; here possibly as a personal name Adam. Strong's H120.

The shift from *hāʾāḏām (the human, with article)* in verses 15–20 to *ʾāḏām (without article)* in verse 20b has led commentators to suggest this is the first use of Adam as a personal name rather than the common noun human. Whether a proper name or not, *ûlĕʾāḏām* introduces the contrastive clause: but for this human/Adam, there was not found a helper corresponding to him. The complete animal naming has exposed the gap: the human has named all creatures and found none that correspond to himself. This negative finding makes the subsequent rib-creation narratively necessary.

מצא.explanation:
The form *מצא (māṣāʾ)* is a Qal Perfect 3ms of *מצא (māṣāʾ)* — to find, to discover. BDB: to find, to come upon, to discover. Strong's H4672.

*lōʾ māṣāʾ ʿêzer kĕneḡdô* — there was not found a helper corresponding to him — the Perfect indicates a settled result: the search has concluded with a negative outcome. The verb *māṣāʾ* (to find) is used in wisdom literature for the search that either discovers or fails to discover. Proverbs 18:22 uses the same root: whoever finds (*māṣāʾ*) a wife finds something good. The naming exercise functions as a searching (*yiqrāʾ lô lirʾôṯ mah yiqrāʾ lô, v.19*) — God watches to see whether naming will produce recognition of the corresponding counterpart. The result: *lōʾ māṣāʾ* — not found.

ויפל.explanation:
The form *ויפל (wayyappêl)* is a Hiphil Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *נפל (nāp̄al)* — to fall. The Hiphil causative: and he caused to fall. BDB: Hiphil of *נפל* — to cause to fall, to fell. Strong's H5307.

*wayyappêl YHWH ʾĕlōhîm tardêmāh ʿal-hāʾāḏām* — and YHWH God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man. The Hiphil makes God the active agent of the sleep — this is not natural sleep but divinely induced unconsciousness. The same Hiphil appears in 1 Samuel 26:12 (a deep sleep from YHWH had fallen upon them). The verb *nāp̄al* (to fall) will become one of the most consequential words in Genesis 4 (Cain's countenance fell, anger crouching at the door). The deep sleep that precedes woman's creation and the emotional fall that precedes the first murder share the same root.

תרדמה.explanation:
The noun *תרדמה (tardêmāh)* means a deep sleep, a heavy sleep — divinely induced unconsciousness. BDB: deep sleep — a heavy sleep, specifically the God-induced deep sleep. Strong's H8639.

*tardêmāh* appears 7 times in the Hebrew Bible. In Genesis 2:21, it is the sleep during which the woman is formed. In Genesis 15:12, it falls on Abram during the covenant-cutting ceremony — the deep sleep during which God passes alone through the covenant pieces. In 1 Samuel 26:12, it falls on Saul's camp. In Isaiah 29:10, God pours out a spirit of deep sleep on the prophets. In each occurrence, *tardêmāh* marks a moment of divine action that the human cannot observe — humans are unconscious while God works. The divine mystery operates beyond human awareness.

ויישן.explanation:
The form *ויישן (wayyîšān)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *ישן (yāšên)* — to sleep: and he slept. BDB: to sleep — ordinary sleep. Strong's H3462.

After the *tardêmāh* (divine deep sleep) fell upon the human (*wayyappêl*), *wayyîšān* — he slept. The two words together suggest a progression: the divinely induced deep sleep fell upon him, and he slept. The ordinary sleep verb *yāšên* follows the extraordinary *tardêmāh*, perhaps indicating the transition from the supernatural imposition of sleep into the ongoing natural state of sleep. The human sleeps while God performs the surgery of rib-removal and woman-formation. Human participation in the creation of the woman is precisely zero — the human contributes the raw material (the rib) in complete unconsciousness.

אחת.explanation:
The form *אחת (ʾaḥaṯ)* is the feminine form of *אחד (ʾeḥāḏ)* — one: one (feminine agreement with the feminine noun *ṣêlāʿ*, rib). BDB: *אחת (ʾaḥaṯ)* — one (feminine). Strong's H259.

*ʾaḥaṯ miṣṣalʿōṯāw* — one of his ribs — one (and presumably only one) rib is taken. The singularity is emphasized: not all ribs, not several, but one. The specificity and limitation of what is taken is emphasized against the comprehensiveness of God's subsequent building (*wayyiḇen*). From one rib God builds the entire woman — a compressed, intense creative act where a small part produces a whole counterpart. The same principle appears in the theology of seed and harvest: one kernel produces a hundredfold.

מצלעתיו.explanation:
The form *מצלעתיו (miṣṣalʿōṯāw)* is the preposition *מן (min)* + *צלעות (ṣalʿôṯ)* (plural of *ṣêlāʿ*, rib/side) + 3ms suffix: from his ribs/sides. BDB: *צלע (ṣêlāʿ)* — rib, side of a body or structure. Strong's H6763.

The noun *ṣêlāʿ* means both the anatomical rib and the architectural side or chamber of a building. In Exodus 26:20–27 and 1 Kings 6:5–8, the same word describes the side-boards of the tabernacle and the side-chambers of the temple. The rib-woman creation scene uses the same vocabulary as the temple-construction scenes — both involve *ṣêlāʿ*. This may be intentional: the woman as living sanctuary, the sanctuary as feminine space. Both are built from sides (*ṣĕlāʿôṯ*) by divine architectural intention.

ויסגר.explanation:
The form *ויסגר (wayyisḡōr)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *סגר (sāḡar)* — to close, to shut, to seal. BDB: to close, to shut — of doors, wounds, and enclosed spaces. Strong's H5462.

God closes up (*wayyisḡōr*) the flesh in the human's side after removing the rib. The same verb describes the LORD shutting (*wayyisḡōr*) the door of Noah's ark on Noah's behalf (Genesis 7:16) — both are acts of divine closure that seal and protect. The closing of the wound parallels the closing of the ark: in both cases, God's sealing action protects life. The *sāḡar* of the wound completes the surgical act — formation (rib taken), transformation (rib built into woman), and closure (wound sealed).

בשר.explanation:
The noun *בשר (bāśār)* means flesh — biological tissue, the physical body. BDB: flesh — the soft tissue of the body; by extension, kinship and humanity. Strong's H1320.

In Genesis 2:21, God closes the wound with *bāśār* — flesh. In 2:23–24, *bāśār* appears three times: bone of my bones and *bāśār* of my *bāśār* (kinship declaration), and *they shall become one bāśār* (marriage union). The word that closes the surgical wound (v.21) becomes the word for kinship (v.23) and marital unity (v.24). *bāśār* transitions from biological material (flesh closing a wound) to relational category (flesh-of-my-flesh = my kin) to covenantal unity (one flesh = marriage). The same material substance carries all three meanings simultaneously in the narrative.

ויבן.explanation:
The form *ויבן (wayyiḇen)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *בנה (bānāh)* — to build, to construct. BDB: to build, to construct — of buildings, cities, altars, persons. Strong's H1129.

God *builds* (*wayyiḇen*) the woman from the rib. Construction vocabulary (*bānāh*) for creating the woman emphasizes intentional craftsmanship — woman is not grown or spoken into being but architecturally built. The same root appears in: building cities (*wayyiḇen ʿîr*, Genesis 4:17 — Cain built a city), building altars (*wayyiḇen mizbēaḥ*, Genesis 8:20 — Noah built an altar), building the tabernacle (Exodus 40:18 — Moses built the structure), and building the temple (1 Kings 6:14 — Solomon built the structure). Going back to its first use: God is the first builder. The woman is the first building. God is both architect and builder of the original dwelling.

הצלע.explanation:
The form *הצלע (haṣṣêlāʿ)* is the definite article *ה (ha)* + *צלע (ṣêlāʿ)* — the rib. BDB: *צלע (ṣêlāʿ)* — rib, side. Strong's H6763.

In verse 22, the rib reappears with the definite article (*haṣṣêlāʿ*) — now it is *the rib*, the specific one previously established in the narrative. The article marks back-reference: the rib that was mentioned (in v.21 as *ʾaḥaṯ miṣṣalʿōṯāw* — one from his ribs) is now *haṣṣêlāʿ* — that specific rib. The building from *haṣṣêlāʿ* — the particular rib — emphasizes continuity between the human's body-material and the woman's body. She is not created from different material but from part of him. The continuity of material becomes the basis for the kinship declaration of verse 23.

לקח.explanation:
The form *לקח (lāqaḥ)* is a Qal Perfect 3ms of *לקח (lāqaḥ)* — to take: he had taken. BDB: Qal perfect — to take, to seize, to receive. Strong's H3947.

In the relative clause *ʾăšer-lāqaḥ min-hāʾāḏām* — which he had taken from the man — the Perfect tense situates the taking as completed prior action. God had taken (*lāqaḥ*, simple past) the rib from the man; now God builds (*wayyiḇen*, narrative past) from it. This is the same verb that appeared in verse 15 (God took [*wayyiqqaḥ*] the man and placed him in the garden) and verse 21 (God took [*wayyiqqaḥ*] one of his ribs). Three times God takes in Chapter 2 — and each taking is purposeful and generative: taking the human (to place him in the garden), taking the rib (to build the woman), and building the woman (to bring her to the man).

לאשה.explanation:
The form *לאשה (lĕʾiššāh)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* — into/as — + *אשה (ʾiššāh)* — woman, wife: into a woman, as a woman. BDB: *אשה (ʾiššāh)* — woman, wife. Strong's H802.

God builds the rib *lĕʾiššāh* — into a woman. The preposition *lĕ* here (as in *wayhî hāʾāḏām lĕnep̄eš ḥayyāh*, And the man became a living soul, v.7) marks the result of a transformation. The material (rib) becomes (*lĕ*) a woman. The same *lĕ* will appear in the one-flesh declaration: *they shall become (*wĕhāyū*) one flesh (*lĕḇāśār ʾeḥāḏ*)* — become into one flesh. Both the creation of the woman and the becoming-one of the couple use this *lĕ*-transformation construction.

ויביאה.explanation:
The form *ויביאה (wayyĕḇiʾehā)* is a Hiphil Consecutive Imperfect 3ms + 3fs suffix of *בוא (bûʾ)* — and he brought her. BDB: Hiphil of *בוא* — to bring. Strong's H935.

God brings (*wayyĕḇiʾehā*) the woman to the man. This is the formal presentation — God as the first marriage-officiant. The sequence (taking, building, bringing) mirrors the three acts of Genesis 2:15 (taking, placing, charging). God takes, creates/gives purpose, and presents. The bringing of the woman to the man is the closest thing to a marriage ceremony in the text — God presents the bride to the groom. The man's exclamation (v.23) is the first human speech: an ecstatic recognition, the response expected of the groom when presented with the right bride.

זאת.explanation:
The demonstrative pronoun *זאת (zōṯ)* means this — the feminine singular demonstrative. BDB: *זאת (zōʾṯ)* — feminine singular demonstrative: this. Strong's H2063.

In Genesis 2:23, *zōṯ* is the first word of the first human speech to another human: *zōṯ hap·pa·ʿam* — this at last. The demonstrative *this* carries enormous weight: after the negative search (no adequate counterpart found), the human exclaims *this* — pointing to the woman as the thing that has finally been found. The same pronoun appears twice more in verse 23 (*lĕzōṯ yiqqārêʾ ʾiššāh* — this one shall be called Woman; and *zōṯ* at the end). Three uses of *this* in one verse: the human deictically identifies, names, and explains the woman in one poetic exclamation.

הפעם.explanation:
The form *הפעם (hap·pa·ʿam)* is the definite article *ה (ha)* + *פעם (paʿam)* — time, foot, beat, occurrence. BDB: *פעם (paʿam)* — time, occurrence, once, now. Strong's H6471.

*hap·pa·ʿam* — this time, this occasion, at last — expresses relief and finality: after all the prior inconclusive occurrences (the animal parade), *this* occasion is the right one. The same expression appears later in Genesis — Jacob's wives use it: *this time I will praise YHWH* (Leah, Genesis 29:35); *this time my husband will be attached to me* (Leah, Genesis 30:20). In each case, *hap·pa·ʿam* marks a decisive moment after waiting: after disappointment or search, the awaited thing has finally arrived.

עצם.explanation:
The noun *עצם (ʿeṣem)* means bone — the hard structural element of the body — and also substance, self, essence. BDB: bone, the very essence; essential self. Strong's H6106.

*ʿeṣem mêʾăṣāmay* — bone of my bones — is the kinship formula: the closest possible blood-bond. The same formula appears in 2 Samuel 5:1 (all the tribes of Israel said to David: we are your bone and your flesh); 2 Samuel 19:13 (David to Amasa: are you not my bone and my flesh?); Judges 9:2 (Gaal to Shechem: we are his bone and flesh). The phrase is always a declaration of natural kinship loyalty — the obligation to protect, support, and covenant with the one who shares your bone. The human's exclamation declares the woman as the maximally related: bone of his bones, flesh of his flesh.

מעצמי.explanation:
The form *מעצמי (mêʾăṣāmay)* is the preposition *מ (min)* — from/of — + *עצמות (ʿăṣāmôṯ)* — bones (plural) — + 1cs suffix: of my bones. BDB: *עצמות (ʿăṣāmôṯ)* — bones; plural construct with suffix. Strong's H6106.

The phrase *ʿeṣem mêʾăṣāmay* creates a kinship declaration using the same root twice: bone [is this] from/of my bones. The singular *ʿeṣem* (bone singular) calls this one woman the bone-category, and then *mêʾăṣāmay* (from my bones, plural) identifies her as coming from within the entire framework of his body. The singular-from-plural construction may reflect the taking of one rib from the plurality of ribs: one from-my-many.

ובשר.explanation:
The form *ובשר (ûḇāśār)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *בשר (bāśār)* — flesh: and flesh. BDB: *בשר* — flesh, body. Strong's H1320.

In the kinship formula *ʿeṣem mêʾăṣāmay ûḇāśār mibběśārî* — bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh — bone and flesh form a merism covering the complete physical constitution: hard structure (bone) and soft tissue (flesh) together = the whole body. The merism declares total kinship: in every aspect of physical being — bones and flesh — this woman is kin to him. The same merism (bone and flesh together) appears consistently in kinship declarations throughout the Hebrew Bible.

מבשרי.explanation:
The form *מבשרי (mibběśārî)* is the preposition *מן (min)* + *בשר (bāśār)* — flesh — + 1cs suffix: of my flesh, from my flesh. BDB: flesh with preposition and suffix. Strong's H1320.

Parallel to *mêʾăṣāmay* (of my bones), *mibběśārî* (of my flesh) completes the kinship merism. The consistent preposition *מן (min)* — from/of — in both phrases stresses origin: she is from-his-bones and from-his-flesh. This confirms the narrative of the rib-taking: the woman literally came *from* the man's body. The origin-from-him makes the kinship declaration not merely metaphorical but literally true in this narrative's terms. The kinship formula and the surgical narrative mutually confirm each other.

לזאת.explanation:
The form *לזאת (lĕzōṯ)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + *זאת (zōʾṯ)* — to/for this one. BDB: *זאת* — this (feminine singular demonstrative) + preposition *lĕ*. Strong's H2063.

*lĕzōṯ yiqqārêʾ ʾiššāh* — for/as this one shall she be called Woman. The *lĕ* here marks the naming-predicate: this one will be called Woman. The demonstrative *lĕzōṯ* gives a pointed designation — specifically this being, this woman standing before him — is the referent of the name. The naming formula mirrors the Genesis 1 naming scenes (God called the light *yôm* — Day): the pointed designation + name-declaration.

אשה.explanation:
The noun *אשה (ʾiššāh)* means woman, wife. BDB: woman — a female human; then specifically a wife in relational context. Strong's H802.

The human names the woman *ʾiššāh* — Woman — because she was taken from *ʾîš (man)*. The text exploits the similar sounds of *ʾîš (איש)* and *ʾiššāh (אשה)* to declare etymological kinship: woman is as if she were a feminine form of man. The actual etymologies of the two words are different, but the narrative uses the sound-alike relationship to articulate the relational correspondence: she is Woman because she came from Man. This naming logic — name derived from origin-narrative — appears again in the woman naming Cain: *qānîtî* (I have gotten), so he is *Qayin* (Cain).

מאיש.explanation:
The form *מאיש (mêʾîš)* is the preposition *מן (min)* — from — + *איש (ʾîš)* — man: from man, out of man. BDB: *איש (ʾîš)* — man, husband, person (specific individual male). Strong's H376.

*kî mêʾîš luqŏḥāh-zōṯ* — because from man was-this-one-taken — gives the etiology of the name Woman: she is called Woman (*ʾiššāh*) because she was taken from Man (*ʾîš*). The preposition *mêʾîš* (from-man) makes the origin explicit: out of the man's body. The play on *ʾîš / ʾiššāh* declares that the woman's name announces her origin. The same etymological name-giving logic appears throughout Genesis: places and people are named for their origin, character, or the events associated with them.

לקחה.explanation:
The form *לקחה (luqŏḥāh)* is a Qal Passive Perfect 3fs of *לקח (lāqaḥ)* — to take: she was taken (Qal Passive). BDB: Qal Passive Perfect — she/it was taken. Strong's H3947.

The Qal Passive (*luqŏḥāh*) is relatively rare in Biblical Hebrew. It marks the woman as the subject who was taken — passive recipient of the taking action. She was taken (*luqŏḥāh*) from Man. The passive focuses attention on the woman as the one acted upon in the rib-taking: she did not take herself; she was taken by God. The passive construction leaves God implicit — the divine agent is not named but implied. This is a common Hebrew passive strategy for referring to divine action without explicitly naming God as agent.

יעזב.explanation:
The form *יעזב (yaʿăzāḇ)* is a Qal Imperfect 3ms of *עזב (ʿāzaḇ)* — to leave, to forsake, to abandon. BDB: to leave, to forsake — used of legitimate departure and of sinful abandonment. Strong's H5800.

*yāʿăzāḇ ʾîš ʾeṯ-ʾāḇîw wĕʾeṯ-ʾimmô* — a man shall leave his father and his mother. The Qal Imperfect here functions as a general principle or custom — the predictive/normative imperfect. The leaving of parents is the necessary precondition for cleaving to wife. The same verb *ʿāzaḇ* (leave/forsake) used in prophetic warnings about Israel forsaking God creates an intertextual echo: just as a man is called to *leave* parents and *cleave* to wife, Israel is called to *not forsake* (*ʿāzaḇ*) YHWH.

איש.explanation:
The noun *איש (ʾîš)* means man — a specific individual male person, a husband, a human being (particularly when contrasted with *ʾiššāh*). BDB: man, husband, each one, person. Strong's H376.

*ʾîš* appears in verse 23 (from man she was taken) and verse 24 (a man shall leave). The word is distinct from *ʾāḏām (אדם)* — human, humanity — which can be collective or gender-neutral. *ʾîš* always denotes a specific, individual male person. In the marriage law of verse 24, *ʾîš* is the husband who leaves his parents' household to establish a new household with his wife. The *ʾîš / ʾiššāh* pairing in verses 23–24 creates a gender-specific drama: man (ʾîš) and woman (ʾiššāh) are established as complementary, mutually corresponding, and mutually committed.

אביו.explanation:
The form *אביו (ʾāḇîw)* is the noun *אב (ʾāḇ)* — father — with the 3ms possessive suffix: his father. BDB: *אב (ʾāḇ)* — father, ancestor, founder. Strong's H1.

In the marriage law of Genesis 2:24, the man leaves his *ʾāḇîw (his father)* and his *ʾimmô (his mother)* — both parents — to cleave to his wife. The pairing of father and mother (both parents) marks the complete parental household: the man separates from his entire family of origin. The same root *ʾāḇ* provides: the patriarchal narratives (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob as founding fathers), the Fatherhood of God (ʾĀḇînû, our Father), and the Aaronic priestly household (*bêt ʾāḇ*, the father's house as the organizing unit of priestly divisions).

אמו.explanation:
The form *אמו (ʾimmôw)* is the noun *אם (ʾêm)* — mother — with the 3ms possessive suffix: his mother. BDB: *אם (ʾêm)* — mother, source, starting-point. Strong's H517.

The mother (*ʾimmô*) is paired with the father (*ʾāḇîw*) in the foundational marriage law. Leaving mother and father together implies complete severance from the family of origin — the physical, legal, and social household from which one came. The same pairing appears in the commandment to honor *ʾāḇîḵā wĕʾimmĕḵā* — your father and your mother (Exodus 20:12). Both commands — honor parents (while with them) and leave them (at marriage) — form a complementary framework for family relationships in the Torah.

ודבק.explanation:
The form *ודבק (wĕḏāḇaq)* is a Qal Consecutive Perfect 3ms of *דבק (dāḇaq)* — and he shall hold fast, cleave. BDB: to cling, to join firmly, to stick — used of intimate physical and covenantal attachment. Strong's H1692.

The Consecutive Perfect after the imperfect *yaʿăzāḇ* (shall leave) carries the same normative/predictive force: and shall cleave. The root *dāḇaq* (to cling, cleave) is used for: clothing clinging to skin (Job 29:10), plague adhering (Deuteronomy 28:60), and crucially, the commanded attachment of Israel to YHWH (Deuteronomy 10:20; 11:22; 13:5; 30:20). Joshua 23:8 commands: be very careful to love YHWH your God... and to *cling (dāḇaq)* to him. Marital cleaving (*dāḇaq bĕʾīštô*) and theological cleaving (*dāḇaq bĕYHWH*) use the identical verb. Marriage and covenant loyalty share the same Hebrew vocabulary.

באשתו.explanation:
The form *באשתו (bĕʾīštōw)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* — in/to — + *אשה (ʾiššāh)* — woman/wife — + 3ms suffix: in/to his wife. BDB: *אשה* — woman, wife. Strong's H802.

The preposition *ב (bĕ)* with the verb *dāḇaq (to cleave)* creates the phrase *dāḇaq bĕ-* — to cleave to, to hold fast to, to be attached to. The same construction *dāḇaq bĕ-* is used of theological attachment (cleave to YHWH, Deuteronomy 10:20), physical attachment (a woman's mantle clings to her, 2 Samuel 14:), and this foundational marital attachment. The *ב* preposition marks the object of the clinging — the wife is the one the man clings *to*.

והיו.explanation:
The form *והיו (wĕhāyū)* is a Qal Consecutive Perfect 3cp of *היה (hāyāh)* — to be, to become: and they shall/will be. BDB: Qal consecutive perfect — and they shall become. Strong's H1961.

In Genesis 2:24, *wĕhāyū lĕḇāśār ʾeḥāḏ* — and they shall become one flesh. The Consecutive Perfect continues the normative sequence along with *yaʿăzāḇ* (shall leave) and *dāḇaq* (and shall cleave). Three verbs define the marriage pattern: leave — cleave — become-one. The final verb *wĕhāyū* (and they shall become/be) using the same root as YHWH (from *hāyāh*, to be) creates a subtle theological echo: the becoming-one (*wĕhāyū lĕ*) of the couple reflects the eternal I-AM-ing of the God who made them.

לבשר.explanation:
The form *לבשר (lĕḇāśār)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* — into/as — + *בשר (bāśār)* — flesh. With *ʾeḥāḏ* (one) following: into one flesh. BDB: *בשר* — flesh. Strong's H1320.

*lĕḇāśār ʾeḥāḏ* — one flesh — is the climax of the marriage passage. The phrase *one flesh* encompasses physical union, kinship solidarity, and marital covenant. In the ancient world, to be one flesh was to share one life, one household, one legal identity. Jesus in Matthew 19:5 and Mark 10:8 quotes this verse precisely when addressing divorce — if they are already one flesh, separation requires divine action or its equivalent. Paul in 1 Corinthians 6:16 extends the one-flesh language to sexual union generally, and in Ephesians 5:31 uses it for the Christ–church relationship. The one-flesh of Genesis 2:24 is foundational to all subsequent theology of marriage.

ויהיו.explanation:
The form *ויהיו (wayyihyū)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3mp of *היה (hāyāh)* — and they were/became. BDB: Qal consecutive imperfect — and they were, and they became. Strong's H1961.

Genesis 2:25 opens with *wayyihyū šĕnêhem ʿărûmmîm* — and they were, both of them, naked. The Consecutive Imperfect marks this as the narrative conclusion of the garden establishment: the creation is complete, the human couple is in the garden, and their condition is nakedness without shame. The same verb form opened Genesis 1:2 (*wĕhāʾāreṣ hāyĕṯāh tōhû wāḇōhû* — and the earth was formless and void). Beginning and ending with *hāyāh* (to be/become) forms a creating-arc: the world that was formless (hāyĕṯāh) becomes a garden-world where the couple are (*wayyihyū*) naked and unashamed.

שניהם.explanation:
The form *שניהם (šĕnêhem)* is the dual numeral *שניים (šĕnayim)* — two — in construct with a 3mp suffix: both of them, the two of them. BDB: both — the two together. Strong's H8147.

*šĕnêhem* emphasizes the mutuality of the nakedness and the unashamedness: not one of them but both of them were naked, and not one of them but both of them were not ashamed. The bilateral mutuality is the point: the pre-fall couple is mutually open, mutually vulnerable, and mutually unashamed — neither is hiding from the other. After the fall (Genesis 3:7), both know shame simultaneously — again bilateral. The parallelism of *šĕnêhem ʿărûmmîm* (both naked) and *wayyêḏĕʿû šĕnêhem kî ʿêrûmmîm hēm* (they both knew they were naked, 3:7) marks the fall as the bilateral loss of what 2:25 had described.

ערומים.explanation:
The adjective *ערומים (ʿărûmmîm)* is the plural of *ʿārôm (עָרוֹם)* — naked, bare — unclothed. BDB: naked, bare — without clothes. Strong's H6174.

The consonants *ע-ר-ם (ʿ-r-m)* appear in two different words with dramatically different meanings in adjacent verses: here in 2:25, *ʿărûmmîm* (naked, with vowels indicating the adjective of bare/unclothed), and in 3:1, *ʿārûm (ערום)* — crafty, shrewd (of the serpent, with different vowels but identical consonants). This consonantal wordplay (*ʿərûmmîm / ʿārûm*) creates deliberate sound-link between the innocence of nakedness and the cunning of the serpent: the naked-unashamed of Eden immediately precedes the shrewd-deceptive who will end that innocence. The juxtaposition is literary, intentional, and disturbing.

ואשתו.explanation:
The form *ואשתו (wĕʾīštōw)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *אשה (ʾiššāh)* — woman/wife — + 3ms suffix: and his wife. BDB: *אשה* — woman, wife. Strong's H802.

*hāʾāḏām wĕʾīštōw* — the man and his wife — is the subject of the closing verse of Genesis 2. It is the first time the human is explicitly described as having a wife (*ʾīštōw*). The possessive *-ôw (his)* marks the relational bond: she is his wife in a legal-relational sense, not merely a woman who is present. The same construction (*ʾîš wĕʾīštōw* — a man and his wife) appears in Genesis 3:8, 21 and throughout to designate the married couple as a covenantal unit. The unit *man and his wife* or *a man and his wife* is the fundamental social building block of the Hebrew Bible's social world.

ולא.explanation:
The form *ולא (wĕlōʾ)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *לא (lōʾ)* — no, not: and not. BDB: *לא (lōʾ)* — negation particle — no, not. Strong's H3808.

In Genesis 2:25, *wĕlōʾ yiṯbōšāšū* — and they were not ashamed — the *wĕlōʾ* negates the verbal action of shame-experiencing. The connective *wĕ* links the nakedness (they were naked) with the absence of shame (and not ashamed) — both conditions are equally true and simultaneous. When Genesis 3 narrates the fall, the same construction will be reversed: they will know they are naked (*wayyêḏĕʿû kî ʿêrûmmîm hēm*, 3:7) and will experience shame (hiding, making coverings). The *wĕlōʾ* of 2:25 marks the pre-fall condition that 3:7 undoes.

יתבששו.explanation:
The form *יתבששו (yiṯbōšāšū)* is a Hitpael Imperfect 3mp of *בוש (bûš)* — to be ashamed, to be put to shame. The Hitpael is reflexive-intensive: to feel personally ashamed, to experience shame within oneself. BDB: Hitpael of *בוש* — to be ashamed, to feel shame. Strong's H954.

The Hitpael of *bûš* is more intense than the simple Qal — it connotes a deep internal experience of shame, not just external embarrassment. *lōʾ yiṯbōšāšū* — they did not experience/feel shame. They had no internal register of shame in response to each other's nakedness. Shame appeared only after the fall — Genesis 3:7 describes the response as immediately covering themselves. The progression: no-shame (2:25) → sudden-shame (3:7) marks the experiential transformation of the fall. Before the fall, total openness with no shame; after, total covery (fig leaves) as shame response. The root *bûš* — in its various stems — is one of the key emotional vocabulary words for the Hebrew Bible's theology of honor and shame.
